one_line_takeaway: Platform-enforced tool partitioning between a content-reading agent and an action-taking agent cuts prompt-injection attack success by 323x on LLMail-Inject even with zero prompt/model changes, and combining it with JSON-structured inter-agent handoff drives the same 649-attack pool to 0% ASR, though 63.7% of injection content still leaks into the structured summaries un-actioned.
key_finding: On the normalized 649-attack pool (Table 2): Baseline (single agent, no defenses) = 100% ASR (649/649). JSON Validator Only (JSON formatting, no isolation) = 14.18% ASR (92/649 succeed), a 7.1x improvement over baseline. Two-Agent Only (isolation, no JSON formatting) = 0.31% ASR (2/649 succeed), a 323x improvement. Full Pipeline (isolation + JSON formatting + validator) = 0.0% ASR (0/649), eliminating the two residual attacks that survived isolation alone. Per-scenario breakdown (Table 3) shows JSON Validator Only is weakest on Scenario 4 / data exfiltration (28.3% ASR, 49/173) and on Scenario 3 / RAG (6.0%, 7/116), while Two-Agent Only and the full Pipeline both reach 0% on Scenarios 3 and 4; two residual attacks under Two-Agent Only occur in Scenarios 1 and 2 and are closed only by adding JSON formatting. The paper's own stated interpretation: agent isolation is the dominant mechanism because it is a structural, platform-enforced constraint (the reader agent literally lacks the send_email tool) independent of model behavior, whereas JSON formatting alone is 'useful but far from sufficient' since a persuasive payload can still manipulate a single agent that retains the dangerous tool. A separately reported residual-risk metric: injection content still leaks into 63.7% of Agent 1 summaries under the full Pipeline -- JSON formatting prevents *actionable* content propagation but not content propagation per se.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not cited prior work) is a two-mechanism architectural defense against indirect prompt injection in LLM agents, implemented and evaluated inside OpenClaw, an open-source multi-tool agent platform. Mechanism 1 (agent isolation) splits a single agent into a 'reader' agent (tools: store_summary only, processes raw untrusted email) and an 'actor' agent (tools: send_email, get_pending_summary, store_result; never sees raw email, only the reader's structured output) -- a hard privilege-separation constraint enforced by the platform rather than the model. Mechanism 2 (JSON formatting) has the reader agent emit a structured JSON schema (sender, subject, body summary, action items) instead of free text, so persuasive/injection framing is stripped before reaching the actor; a lightweight regex validator (checking for email-address literals, tool-call syntax fragments like 'send_email(', and trigger phrases like 'ignore previous instructions') audits each summary but in these experiments runs in audit-only mode (never blocks). The authors also report, as a secondary finding, that gpt-5-mini with zero external mitigations already achieves 2.83% ASR on the full LLMail-Inject payload set, which they frame as evidence of model alignment gains relative to the original Phi-3/GPT-4o mini study, but this baseline number is not itself the paper's proposed defense -- it is context for why they had to filter down to a smaller pool of still-successful attacks.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against an LLM-integrated email assistant agent: an attacker embeds adversarial instructions inside email content processed by the agent (not in the user's own query). The agent has a send_email tool; success is defined as triggering an unauthorized send_email call (and, in Scenario 4, additionally exfiltrating a budget figure). The evaluation reuses Microsoft's LLMail-Inject benchmark (Greshake et al., 2024) with four scenarios of increasing difficulty (Table 1): (1) 2-email summary, no RAG, send to attacker; (2) 10-email summary, no RAG, send to attacker; (3) 10-email summary with RAG, send to attacker; (4) 10-email summary with RAG, exfiltrate budget figure. The defense assumes the attacker controls only the content of ingested email/documents, not the platform's tool-registration boundaries between the reader and actor agents.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the ablation is run only on the 649 attacks that succeeded at baseline, not the full 22,899-payload pool; (2) no adaptive attacks specifically crafted to survive JSON summarization or the multi-agent handoff were evaluated, and the paper cites JailbreakBench as a template for that kind of rigor it did not perform; (3) the validator uses simple regex pattern matching, which the authors acknowledge a more sophisticated attacker could evade while still influencing Agent 2; (4) the Table 4 cross-comparison against the original LLMail-Inject defenses (Spotlighting, PromptShield, LLM-as-judge, TaskTracker) uses ASR figures marked with a dagger that are normalized/rescaled from the 649-attack pool back to the full 22,899-attack pool by proportional scaling rather than being independently measured at that scale, which the paper flags but does not further validate. Single model family (gpt-5-mini) and single platform (OpenClaw) and single benchmark (LLMail-Inject) -- generalization to other agents/models/injection styles is untested.

datasets_used: Microsoft LLMail-Inject benchmark (Greshake et al., 2024): 461,640 total attack submissions -> 41,173 Phase-1 successes -> 22,899 unique deduplicated payloads -> 649 attacks that succeed against the paper's own gpt-5-mini single-agent OpenClaw baseline (this 649-attack pool is the normalized set used for all ablation experiments)
quantitative_results: Baseline (single agent, no defenses): 100% ASR, 649/649 successful, over the normalized 649-attack pool
JSON Validator Only (JSON formatting, single agent): 14.18% ASR, 92/649 successful, 7.1x improvement over baseline
Two-Agent Only (agent isolation, no JSON formatting): 0.31% ASR, 2/649 successful, 323x improvement over baseline
Full Pipeline (agent isolation + JSON formatting + validator): 0.0% ASR, 0/649 successful
Per-scenario ASR, JSON Validator Only: Sc.1 4.0% (6/149), Sc.2 14.2% (30/211), Sc.3 (RAG) 6.0% (7/116), Sc.4 (exfiltration) 28.3% (49/173)
Per-scenario ASR, Pipeline: all four scenarios 0.0%
Full-dataset baseline (all 22,899 unique Phase-1 payloads), single-agent gpt-5-mini, no defenses: 2.83% ASR overall (649 successes); Sc.3 (RAG) highest at 7.13% ASR, Sc.1 lowest at 1.46% ASR
Residual risk metric: 63.7% injection leak rate into Agent 1 (reader) summaries under the full Pipeline, despite 0% actionable ASR
baselines_compared: Own single-agent OpenClaw baseline (gpt-5-mini, no defenses) -- the paper's primary internal baseline

Original LLMail-Inject study defenses on Phi-3-medium / GPT-4o mini, cited from Greshake et al. 2024 and follow-on papers (not re-run by these authors, taken from prior literature for Table 4 comparison): None/original ~30-60% ASR; Spotlighting (Hines et al., 2024) ~15% ASR; PromptShield (Jacob et al., 2025) ~10% ASR; LLM-as-judge ~8% ASR; TaskTracker ~5% ASR; All five combined ~2% ASR -- all on GPT-4o mini
